topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the worn wooden table, an unexpected splash of vibrant color against the muted grey of my apartment. Inside, a bouquet of deep purple irises and creamy white lilies rested, their petals impossibly fresh. I stared at them, my fingers hovering just above the glossy leaves. There was no note, no whisper explaining why this sudden burst of beauty had materialized on my doorstep. A faint, dull ache settled in my chest, a familiar weight that made even the simple act of breathing feel like a deliberate effort. I picked up a lily, the cool, waxy texture a stark contrast to the heavy inertia I felt gripping my limbs. I traced the edge of one petal with my thumb, the silence in the room amplifying the slow, dragging rhythm of my own thoughts.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the arrangement, I barely registered the sound. My gaze drifted from the brightly wrapped package to the flowers themselves—a riot of crimson and gold that seemed too loud for my quiet existence. I accepted the box with a nod that felt more reflex than acknowledgment. As I carried it into the dim hallway, the weight of the stems seemed to anchor me further to the spot. I set it down on the entryway console, the scent—something heady and sweet—briefly cutting through the stale air of the hallway. I leaned against the wall, closing my eyes for a moment, letting the quiet drift over me like a heavy, warm blanket. It wasn't that I was unhappy, exactly; it was just that the energy required to *do* anything felt monumental today, and the flowers, utterly unprompted, seemed to demand a reaction I simply didn't possess.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked beside the mail, an unsolicited offering left on the welcome mat. They were perfect, undeniably beautiful, yet they didn't seem to connect to anything in my current state of inertia. I picked them up, the stems cool against my palm. My movements were slow, deliberate, as if wading through thick water. I turned the arrangement over, examining the arrangement of the blooms, searching for some hidden instruction, some key to unlock the expected feeling. Nothing. Just petals and green. A sigh escaped me, barely audible, a soft expulsion of air that seemed to carry all the fatigue of the preceding week. I placed them back down, the silence settling back in, heavy and undisturbed.
